abstract:
This study identifies the essential knowledge and competencies required for effective executive leadership in 21st-century higher education, as perceived by sitting university presidents. Through qualitative interviews with thirteen presidents who hold doctoral degrees in higher education administration, the research explores the professional requirements of a role that has become increasingly complex, symbolic, and managerially demanding.

The findings categorize essential knowledge into three primary domains: foundational knowledge (including the history of higher education and finance), the acquisition of cultural knowledge (contextual awareness and public image management), and complex cognitive thinking. In terms of competencies, respondents emphasized the importance of personal attributes such as vision and humility, specialized management skills (spanning assessment, enrollment, fundraising, and strategic planning), and multifaceted communication abilities. The data reveal that modern presidents must function simultaneously as academic leaders and corporate-style chief executives to successfully navigate diverse constituencies and institutional pressures. These results provide a framework for refining doctoral curricula, informing professional development programs, and guiding boards of trustees in the selection and preparation of future university leaders.
